measurement.notes: Expect nonlinear associations; measure continuously and test for U-shaped patterns.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p; also consider quadratic terms or splines in modeling

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Privacy-sensitive; obtain explicit consent and explain aggregation; do not collect content.

feasibility.notes: Depends on OS permissions and app-level aggregation availability.

edges.notes: Better subjective sleep last night expected to reduce next-day overwhelm.
Self-compassion practice expected to reduce guilt quickly.
Perceived validation/support reduces perceived entrapment.
Additional respite hours directly lower entrapment and overwhelm.
Cognitive reappraisal reduces immediate self-criticism.
Activity bursts may reduce tearfulness within hours.
Screen time may have U-shaped effects on tearfulness (short contact vs prolonged passive browsing).

expected_collinearity_risks: P03 (self-compassion) vs P04 (reappraisal): moderate collinearity of adaptive emotion-regulation skill use.
P01 (subjective sleep quality) vs P02 (objective sleep minutes): high collinearity expected; choose single or orthogonalize if necessary.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C02 (positive bond) may show ceiling effects (responses clustered toward high values); consider modeling limited variance.
P06 (respite hours) may be zero-inflated leading to floor effects on many days.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1-9 Likert anchored scales (already applied) to increase within-person variance.
Encourage passive data capture to add continuous variance (steps, screen-time).
If C01 shows too little variance, treat as slow-moving covariate or collect weekly identity-reflection items.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Pharmacotherapy and invasive neuromodulation (e.g., TMS) deprioritized for initial within-person gVAR exploration due to complexity and need for clinical oversight.
Highly overlapping self-regulation subscales were avoided to reduce collinearity (kept one self-compassion and one cognitive reappraisal measure).
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 28 days, 6 EMA prompts/day (168 momentary points) + 28 daily diaries and passive streams, there is sufficient temporal resolution for single-subject gVAR with 8 criterion and 8 predictor nodes after preprocessing. Recommend person-mean centering, detrending for slow-moving variables, orthogonalizing or selecting between highly collinear signals (e.g., subjective vs objective sleep) to improve estimation, and handling missingness with interpolation or multiple imputation tailored to time-series.

safety_notes: No immediate acute self-harm or psychosis signals in the complaint text. Monitor responses for emergent safety risk (explicit suicidal ideation, intent, or severe functioning loss); if reported, encourage immediate contact with local emergency services or a clinician. For questions about resource scarcity (childcare), provide referral options. Passive data collection requires explicit consent and clear privacy explanations.

model_summary: Target seven complaint-derived criteria (acute somatic episodes, unpredictability distress, threat appraisals, low acceptance of medical reassurance, avoidance of leaving home, interoceptive hypervigilance, and functional/emotional impact) and eight modifiable predictors drawn from high-ranking psycho-behavioral levers (cognitive reappraisal, interoceptive exposure, distress-tolerance breathing, graded/behavioral exposure, sleep routine, physical activity, objective autonomic arousal, and psychoeducation engagement). Sampling emphasizes momentary EMA (5 prompts/day) for fast-changing experience and daily diaries + passive phone/wearable streams for behaviors and physiology. Design balances within-person variance, feasibility, and low redundancy to produce an estimable initial gVAR-ready dataset.
variable_selection_notes: I prioritized constructs that directly map to the complaint decomposition (threat appraisal, interoceptive attention, avoidance, somatic intensity, unpredictability distress, response to reassurance, functional impact). To minimize collinearity I selected one broad self-regulation skill (cognitive reappraisal) rather than multiple overlapping cognitive constructs, and one behavioral activation/exposure predictor instead of many activity subtypes. Predictors emphasize psycho-behavioral, low-risk interventions flagged by the HyDe and LLM mapping (exposure, interoceptive work, psychoeducation, distress tolerance, sleep hygiene) and a small set of passive signals (steps, GPS distance-from-home, HR-spike counts) to objectively capture avoidance and arousal. Uncertainty / bias notes: passive wearable signals may be missing for some participants (coverage bias); some predictor–criterion links (e.g., sleep -> threat appraisal) are weaker/indirect and scored lower. Trade-offs: fewer predictors improves gVAR identifiability but may miss niche mechanisms (e.g., neuromodulation), which can be added later if initial signals justify it.

momentary_ema.notes: Five prompts/day balances measurement density to capture short-term dynamics (0–6h/0–24h lags) while limiting burden. Blocks include jitter to reduce predictability and response batching.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures last-night sleep and previous-day avoidance/impact summary (reduces redundancy with momentary EMA).

passive_streams.notes: Continuous passive capture (when available) aggregated to per-prompt windows (since last prompt) and daily summaries to align with EMA windows; includes GPS-derived max distance-from-home per day and per-prompt, daily step counts, and wearable HR-spike counts per EMA window.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Aim for N_obs >= ~100 per person for 1st-order gVAR stability; with 140 momentary prompts + 28 diaries and passive aggregates the expected observation count per variable is sufficient for initial gVAR. Preprocess: within-person detrending (linear/time-of-day), person-mean centering and unit scaling (z-score) for continuous/Likert measures. For bounded Likert variables, use within-person z-scoring; for skewed passive counts (steps, HR spikes) consider log1p or square-root transforms. Check stationarity visually and with augmented Dickey-Fuller per participant; include time-of-day fixed effects or periodic splines if rhythmicity is observed.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Momentary EMA items (1–9 Likert) expected to show medium–high within-person variability (C01, C02, C03, C06). Passive signals (steps, GPS) offer higher objective variance for behavioral endpoints (C05). Daily diaries provide lower-frequency signals useful for longer-lag (24h) effects (C04, C05, C07).
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Main risks: EMA nonresponse (fatigue) and wearable absence. Mitigation: brief items (single 1–9 questions), prompt reminders, optional passive-only fallback (reduce EMA frequency if adherence < 50%), imputation strategies and missingness modelling (MAR assumptions verification).

criteria_variables.label: Acute somatic symptom intensity and frequency

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how intense were any stomach cramps, nausea, heart racing, or dizziness at their worst? (Rate the worst intensity you felt)
measurement.notes: Use single worst-intensity rating per window; optionally add an event flag if an episode occurred (yes/no) for secondary analyses.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; event counts log1p if used

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did unpredictable symptoms (coming without warning) make you feel distressed or upset?
measurement.notes: Captures momentary intolerance/distress about unpredictability; sensitive to skill use (predictors).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you interpret bodily sensations as indicating something dangerous (e.g., 'this means something is wrong')?
measurement.notes: Target for cognitive reappraisal and psychoeducation predictors; expected to respond within hours to cognitive skill use.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

criteria_variables.label: Low acceptance of medical reassurance / perceived invalidation

measurement.response_scale_or_unit: 1-9 Likert (1=made it worse/none, 9=greatly reduced concern) — note: for model consistent polarity we will invert so higher = worse (low acceptance)
measurement.notes: Because reassurance events are relatively rare, the item measures perceived impact; considered lower-frequency but important for functional trust modelling.

measurement.notes: Supported by passive GPS-derived max_distance_from_home (daily) to provide objective validation and to separate perceived avoidance vs actual movement.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; GPS distance log1p for skew

measurement.notes: Direct target of interoceptive exposure and psychoeducation; likely to fluctuate with practice and arousal.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

criteria_variables.label: Functional and emotional impact (annoyance, fear-driven interference)

measurement.item_or_signal: Overall yesterday, how much did symptoms or related behaviors (avoidance, worry) interfere with your daily activities or cause distress?
measurement.notes: Daily capture of overall impact reduces redundancy with high-frequency momentary items.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

predictor_variables.label: Cognitive reappraisal / threat-interpretation reframing (skill use)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Cognitive reappraisal reduces catastrophic threat appraisals and downstream anxiety, decreasing avoidance and functional impact.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Practice labeling thoughts and reframing 'danger' interpretations to benign explanations; short guided reappraisal exercises via app.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you use a cognitive reappraisal or reframe (e.g., reinterpreting sensations as non-dangerous)?
measurement.notes: Records both use and intensity of cognitive skill; useful for temporal coupling with threat appraisals.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

Reducing threat appraisals reduces distress about unpredictability over short-to-medium lags.
Sustained reappraisal can reduce functional impact over longer windows.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Safe; ensure guidance avoids invalidating user experience while encouraging reinterpretation.

feasibility.notes: Simple self-report item; expected stable compliance with momentary EMA.

predictor_variables.label: Interoceptive exposure / somatic experiential practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Planned, guided interoceptive exposures (e.g., intentionally eliciting sensations in safe context) aim to recalibrate sensitivity and reduce catastrophic appraisals and hypervigilance.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short guided exercises (e.g., light hyperventilation, spinning chair when safe) under instruction or simulated exposures like holding breath exercises — done only with appropriate guidance and contraindications screened.

measurement.notes: Also capture type/count in optional free-text for exploratory analyses. Safety: avoid unsupervised high-risk exposures; include screening before recommending.

Exposure may reduce reported symptom intensity or the perceived peak via learning processes.
Relearning about sensations reduces threat appraisals over days.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Interoceptive exposures should be introduced with clinician oversight when possible; avoid exposures contraindicated by medical conditions.

feasibility.notes: Self-report of practice feasible; adherence may vary.

predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Short breathing or grounding practices rapidly downregulate sympathetic arousal and can reduce episode severity and distress.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Guided diaphragmatic breathing, paced breathing apps, 4-4-6 breathing, grounding via 5 senses.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

Rapid downregulation reduces immediate distress about unpredictability.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Safe; encourage medical clearance if major cardiopulmonary disease.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility and expected within-person variance.

predictor_variables.label: Graded behavioral exposure (leaving-home attempts / activation)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Planned, graded attempts to leave home counter avoidance, promote inhibitory learning, and increase confidence functioning outside home.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short stepwise outings (e.g., to end of street, short walk to nearby store) with pre-specified goals and coping plans.

measurement.response_scale_or_unit: GPS distance in meters (daily); 1-9 Likert for self-report

measurement.notes: GPS-derived objective behavior reduces self-report bias. Privacy: compute distance_from_home on-device or send aggregated metrics only.

predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Used for monitoring and biofeedback; increases self-awareness and guides timing of downregulation exercises.

predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Targeted psychoeducation (normalizing sensations, arousal-safety knowledge) reduces threat interpretations and improves acceptance of reassurance.

measurement.item_or_signal: Number of psychoeducation modules completed yesterday (passive) and self-report rating of how helpful the material was (1-9) in morning diary
measurement.notes: Engagement metric can indicate dose–response relation with reassurance acceptance and threat appraisal.

edges.notes: Reappraisal is expected to reduce immediate threat appraisals within hours.
Interoceptive exposure should reduce interoceptive hypervigilance over hours to days.
Graded exposure increases leaving-home behavior and reduces avoidance over daily lags.
HR spike counts likely co-occur with or precede self-reported somatic episode intensity in the same window.
Better sleep expected to reduce next-day somatic sensitivity and intensity.
Distress-tolerance breathing can rapidly reduce distress about unpredictable symptoms.
Psychoeducation can increase acceptance of reassurance over days.
Interoceptive exposure is expected to reduce perceived intensity via inhibitory learning.
Exposure/activation should decrease functional interference over several days as confidence increases.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 (reappraisal) vs P08 (psychoeducation) — both target threat appraisals; monitor for collinearity and consider combining or orthogonalizing if highly correlated.
C01 (somatic intensity) vs P07 (HR spikes) — objective and subjective measurements correlated; avoid treating both as identical constructs in final model.
C02 (unpredictability distress) vs C03 (threat appraisal) — conceptual overlap; ensure items are distinct and use factor checks.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C04 (reassurance acceptance) may show floor/ceiling effects if user rarely receives medical reassurance; consider weekly supplementary item if ceiling observed.
P07 (HR spikes) may be zero-inflated in participants without wearables or low arousal.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1–9 Likert scaling for EMA items to increase within-person variance.
Capture both subjective (EMA) and objective (GPS, steps, HR spikes) signals for behavioral/physiological constructs to improve signal/noise.
Allow optional event flags (e.g., episode occurred Y/N) plus intensity rating to handle rare but intense events.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Neuromodulation (tES/TMS) and pharmacotherapy are deprioritized at initial observational stage due to complexity, safety, and lower feasibility for ecological momentary sampling.
Multiple overlapping cognitive constructs (e.g., both attention-bias training and broad cognitive restructuring) — selected reappraisal as parsimonious cognitive lever.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: Model with 7 criteria and 8 predictors (15 nodes) with dense repeated-measures (5x daily EMA + daily diaries + passive streams) provides adequate within-person observations (>=100 per variable expected) for 1st-stage gVAR estimation after person-mean centering and detrending. Monitor missingness and apply regularization/penalized VAR estimation if needed.

safety_notes: No acute self-harm or psychosis risk is described in the complaint. Safety guidance: include brief crisis resources in the app and an option to pause exposure tasks and contact clinician if symptoms escalate. Interoceptive exposures should be taught or recommended with screening for medical contraindications. Do not provide medical diagnostic statements or medication dosing; encourage users with new/worsening somatic signs to follow up with their clinician.

model_summary: Targeting nocturnal perseverative cognition and its sleep/circadian consequences, this initial observation model uses 5 complaint-derived criteria (nighttime replay of past conversations, anticipatory worry/list-making, global nighttime cognitive hyperarousal, delayed sleep onset, and morning interpersonal irritability) and 8 modifiable predictors drawn from high-signal circadian and CBTi-style levers (morning bright light exposure, evening screen minutes, evening wind-down adherence, expressive journaling, stimulus-control adherence, scheduled worry-time use, sleep-environment quality, and morning stabilization routine). Measurements combine a single morning diary (captures full-night phenomena and morning state), two evening momentary EMAs (capture evening behaviors/strategies), and passive nightly screen-time aggregation. Design emphasizes 1–9 Likert where appropriate, continuous minutes for behaviors, low redundancy across predictors, and a 3-week collection window to support within-person gVAR while preserving feasibility.
variable_selection_notes: Criteria: all five complaint-derived constructs were retained to preserve complaint resolution and downstream causal paths (replay vs worry vs hyperarousal → sleep onset → morning irritability). Predictors: selected top, actionable nodes prioritized by HyDe and LLM mapping focusing on circadian entrainment (morning light), evening stimulus reduction (screen time, wind-down), cognitive unloading (journaling, scheduled worry), bed/stimulus control, sleep environment, and morning-anchor routines. Trade-offs: limited to 8 predictors to keep gVAR estimable and reduce collinearity; excluded higher-cost/medical nodes (TMS, pharmacotherapy, implantables) at this stage. Bias/uncertainty notes: relevance estimates rely on general CBTi and circadian literature plus ranking signals; individual response heterogeneity and measurement error (self-report vs passive) are expected.

momentary_ema.notes: Two momentary prompts capture evening presleep behaviors/strategies and a daytime check for confounders. Evening prompt targets wind-down routine, journaling, worry-time, and bed-use adherence.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures 'last night' cognitive activity (replay, worry, hyperarousal), sleep onset time, sleep environment rated retrospectively, and current morning irritability.

passive_streams.notes: Passive aggregation of device screen-time minutes in the 2 hours before reported bedtime each night; aggregated per calendar day / night for linkage with morning diary.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Three-week, mixed EMA+passive design yields 63 self-report observations (42 momentary prompts + 21 morning diaries). When passive daily aggregates are included (screen-time) there are 84 measured rows (42 momentary + 21 diaries + 21 passive). With K=13 total variables, note that T (number of usable repeated observations per person for gVAR) is limited: the number of momentary timepoints (42) is below the common heuristic 5*K (65). Consider reducing predictors or extending duration, prioritizing variables, or applying stronger regularization and limiting maximal lag to improve gVAR identifiability.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 Likert for subjective constructs to increase within-person variance. Continuous minutes for screen time and journaling to capture graded effects. Expectable day-to-day variability in nocturnal measures and predictors (esp. screen time, routines).
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Morning diary critical—higher missingness risk on weekends or nights with late sleep. Mitigate with reminder scheduling, make morning diary window broad (e.g., until 10:30) and allow retrospective reporting for previous night. Passive screen-time is robust to missingness but may be unavailable for some OS levels; plan per-subject fallback (self-report evening screen minutes).

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how much did you replay past conversations in your head as if they were happening now?
measurement.notes: Single morning diary item covering the full night's replay episodes to maximize capture of events that occur after evening prompts.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider winsorize at 1–99 percentile if extreme skew

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how often did you generate lists or imagine negative outcomes for the near future (e.g., tomorrow)?
measurement.notes: Captures future-oriented perseverative cognition distinct from past-oriented replay.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how mentally activated or unable to quiet your thoughts did you feel overall?
measurement.notes: Global hyperarousal rating to capture nonspecific activation that sustains replay/worry.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider AR effects (autocorrelation) modeling

measurement.channel: morning_diary (smartphone EMA app); optional passive sleep-detection
measurement.item_or_signal: What time did you fall asleep last night? (enter HH:MM). If unknown, estimate.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: clock time (HH:MM) — will be transformed to minutes-since-midnight and to circular measures
measurement.notes: Prefer self-report sleep onset; augment with passive sleep-onset detection where available to validate.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: convert clock-time to continuous minutes; use circular transform (sine/cosine) or within-person z; evaluate and model daily drift

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how irritated or hostile toward others do you feel?
measurement.notes: Momentary morning affect measure anchored to ~08:00 to capture complaint-specific timing.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

predictor_variables.label: Morning bright light exposure (minutes within 1h of waking)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Morning bright light is a primary circadian cue that advances circadian phase, supports earlier sleep onset over days, and can improve morning mood/alertness.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Spend 10–30 minutes outdoors or next to a bright window within 60 minutes of waking.

measurement.item_or_signal: How many minutes did you spend outdoors or in bright indoor light within the first hour after waking?
measurement.notes: If passive ambient/light sensor available, supplement but self-report is primary.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; clip extreme values (e.g., >180 min)

expected_effects.rationale: Phase-advancing light reduces delayed sleep over days, indirectly reducing replay by improving night timing; effect on past-replay is indirect and slower.
Entrainment reduces evening vulnerability to future-oriented worry through stabilized sleep-wake timing over days.
Better circadian alignment reduces global nighttime hyperarousal across nights.
Consistent morning light exposure tends to advance circadian phase and reduce delayed sleep onset over days to weeks.
Acute morning light and activity can improve immediate morning mood and reduce irritability.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility via morning diary; passive augmentation possible.

predictor_variables.label: Evening screen minutes (minutes of screen-on in 2h before sleep)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Evening screen exposure (light and stimulating content) can increase arousal and delay melatonin onset and sleep onset; screen time minutes provide objective passive measure of this exposure.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Limit screen use or enable full-night blue-light filters in the 2 hours before intended bedtime.

measurement.channel: phone OS screen-time API or smartphone passive logging (minutes of screen-on between bedtime-2h and bedtime)
measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes of screen-on time in the 2 hours before reported bedtime (device-reported).
measurement.recall_window: night prior (2-hour window ending at reported bedtime)

expected_collinearity_risks: P03 (wind-down), P04 (journaling), and P06 (scheduled worry) measure overlapping presleep cognitive-behavioral strategies and may be collinear; consider principal components or penalized gVAR if needed.

excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Neuromodulation (TMS, tES), pharmacotherapy (sleep meds, psychotropics), implantable devices — excluded from initial gVAR due to feasibility, cost, and safety constraints.

diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 5 criteria and 8 predictors measured at daily resolution (2 momentary prompts/day + morning diary + passive daily aggregates) across 21 days, the design yields 42 momentary prompts and 21 morning diaries (63 self-report rows) plus 21 passive daily aggregates. Because the effective repeated timepoints (42 momentary observations) are below the heuristic 5*K (65 for K=13), expect gVAR estimation to require strong regularization, variable selection (drop or combine correlated predictors), or extended monitoring to increase T.

safety_notes: No acute self-harm or psychosis signals are present in the complaint text. If assessments reveal severe worsening (e.g., very high distress, suicidal ideation, or functional impairment), follow local clinical escalation procedures. Encourage participants to contact a health professional if sleep loss or daily functioning becomes severe; provide crisis resources as per platform safety policy.

model_summary: This initial observation model targets recurring nightmare awakenings and their proximate mechanisms: nightmare intensity, anticipatory sleep fear, acute autonomic arousal at awakening, prolonged post-awakening activation, and difficulty returning to sleep. It pairs six criterion variables (captured primarily with a morning sleep-diary and a single nightly pre-sleep EMA) with seven actionable predictors spanning trauma-focused imagery rehearsal, presleep wind-down/routine adherence, post-awakening grounding/paced breathing, sleep-environment optimization, HRV/breathing biofeedback practice + passive HRV, stimulus-control (non-clock checking/bedroom associations), and wearable-based monitoring/feedback. Measures prioritize 1–9 anchored Likert scales to maximize within-person variance, use morning-diary anchors for last-night phenomena, and combine passive wearable signals for objective arousal/awakening markers. The design is conservatively sized for person-level gVAR (daily resolution + 3 momentary prompts) over 28 days to produce sufficient time series while minimizing collinearity and redundancy.
variable_selection_notes: The criteria set preserves the complaint's resolution (nightmares, realistic vividness, anticipatory fear, sweating/heart racing, long calming period, trouble returning to sleep). Chosen predictors emphasize high HyDe/LLM signals (imagery rehearsal, presleep routines, breathing/grounding, environment, HRV biofeedback, stimulus control, and monitoring) that are modifiable and measurable at daily/nightly resolution. Trade-offs: limited number of predictors (7) to keep gVAR estimable while covering major mechanisms (cognitive imagery content, pre-sleep conditioning, autonomic downregulation, environmental contributors, and monitoring/adherence). Redundancy reduced by selecting one broad self-regulation action (presleep wind-down) rather than many overlapping constructs. Uncertainty/bias notes: estimated relevance uses complaint text + ranking signals and domain plausibility; some effects (e.g., environment -> nightmare vividness) are weaker and possibly indirect. Passive HR/HRV may be noisy (device differences) — transformation and quality-control steps are recommended.

time_blocks_local: 08:00-09:30 (morning brief check if not completed diary)
momentary_ema.recall_window: since last prompt / today (for daytime); pre-sleep anchors for bedtime prompt
momentary_ema.notes: Three daily prompts primarily for capturing bedtime anticipatory fear (22:00-23:30), daytime context/adherence, and a fallback morning brief if diary missed. Morning diary is primary for last-night events (see daily_diary).

anchor_times_local: upon_waking (morning diary within 60 minutes of final awakening)
daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures last-night nightmare occurrence, vividness, autonomic signs, time-to-calm, and difficulty resuming sleep. If participant wakes from a nightmare and is willing, an event-contingent brief can be triggered (optional).

passive_streams.notes: Continuous heart rate (and if available HRV estimates), accelerometry/motion, and device-detected sleep/awakening events. Aggregate features produced per night and aligned to morning diary; per-awakening windows (±5 min) flagged for autonomic spikes.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Scheduled observations = 84 EMAs + 28 morning diaries = 112 total. For person-level gVAR, consider that daily-level T=28 (days) is < recommended 3*K (3*13=39) for stable estimation; options: (a) aggregate momentary prompts to daily summaries to increase per-variable T, (b) reduce variable count or (c) use regularized/Bayesian VAR approaches and monitor missingness closely.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 anchored Likert scales for subjective items to increase within-person variance. For time-to-calm use an ordinal 1–9 anchored to minute ranges (e.g., 1=0-2min ... 9=>60min) to avoid heavy right-skew. Passive HR/HRV use log or winsorization for spikes.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Main risks: missed morning diaries after poor sleep and participant burden. Mitigation: short morning diary (5 items), optional event-triggered single-item after nightmare, passive detection to fill gaps, reminder nudges, and accept planned missingness for occasional days.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 Imagery Rehearsal vs P02 Presleep routine: moderate correlation if imagery incorporated into wind-down.
P02 Presleep routine vs P06 Stimulus control: moderate correlation (both capture presleep behaviors).
P05 HRV practice vs P03 grounding/paced breathing: moderate correlation due to overlapping breathing techniques.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: P04 Sleep environment may show low within-person variance if participant does not change environment during study (ceiling).
P07 wearable metrics may have floor effects for HRV if device fails to capture reliable signal.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Encourage small nightly variation (e.g., alternate wind-down activities) to increase within-person variance for P02 and P04.
Use both binary adherence and graded intensity (1–9) to capture partial engagement and increase continuous variance.
Include event-contingent prompts immediately after awakening to reduce recall bias for P03 and C04–C06.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Pharmacotherapy and invasive neuromodulation (e.g., TMS, implantables) — deprioritized for initial within-person gVAR due to complexity, safety and measurement challenges.
Many overlapping self-regulation constructs (e.g., both progressive muscle relaxation and body-scan) — collapsed into presleep routine or breathing practice to avoid collinearity.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: Scheduled observations total 112 (84 EMAs + 28 morning diaries). This gives adequate raw observation count but daily-level T=28 is still < 3*K (39) for K=13 variables; consider aggregating momentary prompts to daily summaries, reducing variable count, or applying regularized/Bayesian VAR modeling to improve estimation stability. Monitor missingness and consider planned missingness strategies.

safety_notes: This model is not a clinical diagnosis. If nightmares escalate, cause functional deterioration, or if there is any emergence of suicidal ideation or safety concerns, advise immediate contact with a mental health professional or emergency services. Imagery and trauma-focused work can be distressing — recommend starting slowly and having a clinician contact option if symptoms worsen. Ensure participant consent and secure handling of biometric data.

model_summary: Select six complaint-derived criteria (difficulty initiating tasks, substituting with low-effort rewards, digital distractibility, loss of time awareness, deadline-triggered panic, and uncertainty/self-blame) and nine modifiable predictors focused on practical, high-signal behavioral levers: implementation intentions, structured focus blocks/timers, digital-distraction (passive phone) metrics, time-awareness checks, contingency/deadline planning (cognitive reappraisal), self-compassion practice, external checklists/offloading, current energy level, and last-night sleep quality. Sampling prioritizes momentary EMA (1–9 Likert) across 6 prompts/day with passive phone aggregation per prompt and a single morning diary for sleep to preserve within-person variance and enable gVAR at short lags (mainly 0–6h, sleep effects 0–24h). The predictor set emphasizes non-redundant, modifiable psychological & environmental strategies with passive triangulation of digital distraction to reduce measurement error.
variable_selection_notes: All six complaint-derived criteria (C01–C06) are retained to preserve full complaint resolution. Predictor selection prioritized high HyDe/LLM signals (implementation intentions, structured focus/micro-tasks, digital distraction reduction, time-estimation training, cognitive restructuring/self-compassion, external offloading/checklists, social/accountability, and sleep/energy) while avoiding many near-duplicate executive-function constructs. Trade-offs: several high-ranked ontology leaves pointed to many overlapping CBT/self-management subnodes; I collapsed close candidates (e.g., task breakdown, graded assignment, and activation design) into 1–2 pragmatic predictors (implementation intentions and structured focus blocks) to limit collinearity and maintain estimability. Passive phone signals were included to objectively capture distraction (reduces reliance on self-report). Uncertainty: social accountability can be beneficial or stress-inducing (nonlinear effects) so its sign is treated cautiously. Missingness and platform-API limitations are the main measurement risks.

momentary_ema.notes: Prompts ask about events/behaviors 'since last prompt' on 1–9 Likert scales to increase within-person variance. Use small jitter inside blocks to avoid predictability.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary collects last-night sleep duration and quality, which are used as a predictor for daytime initiation/attention outcomes (lag 0–24h).

passive_streams.notes: Phone screen_time_minutes and unlock_count aggregated over the past ~3 hours (aligned to each EMA prompt). Ensure privacy (do not capture content) and battery-aware sampling.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Per-person longitudinal data: 28 days × 6 prompts = 168 timepoints (plus 28 morning diaries) = 196 total expected observations. With typical missingness expect ~100–150 usable timepoints; set a realistic minimum target (150) to plan for regularization approaches. Use within-person z-scoring and detrending if multi-day trends appear. Keep variable count moderate: 6 criteria + 9 predictors = 15 nodes; consider shrinkage/GAM-VAR or regularized gVAR for stable estimates and possibly reduce nodes if realized T is much lower.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 anchored Likert for EMA items (increases continuous approximation). Include passive continuous signals (minutes, counts) and daily sleep duration for additional variance. Encourage fine-grained response anchors (e.g., 1 = 'Not at all', 5 = 'Somewhat', 9 = 'Extremely').
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Risk factors: prompt fatigue (6/day), passive-sensor permissions revoked, platform API variations. Mitigations: brief items (<15s), occasional compliance prompts, and incentivize morning diary completion. Monitor and impute with state-space or multiple imputation tuned for time-series.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, when you sat down to begin a planned task, how much did you actually start the intended task within a few minutes? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Immediately and fully)
measurement.notes: Captures initiation intensity/latency as an approximate continuous measure; if a task attempt did not occur, report 1.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person mean-centering and z-scoring; inspect for linear trend across days

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you engage in easier, immediately rewarding activities (e.g., reorganizing photos, watching videos) instead of the task you intended? (1 = Not at all, 9 = A lot)
measurement.notes: If both task and substitutes occurred, rate relative dominance of substitutes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-scoring; log1p not needed

criteria_variables.label: Susceptibility to distraction by digital/novel stimuli

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how often did your attention get captured by phones/apps/novel stimuli interfering with the intended task? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Constantly)
measurement.notes: Triangulate with passive phone metrics (screen time and pickups) aggregated per prompt.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person standardization; consider log transform for skew in passive corroborates

criteria_variables.label: Loss of time awareness during off-task activity

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you lose track of time while doing other activities (i.e., time passed faster than expected)? (1 = Not at all, 9 = A great deal)
measurement.notes: Combine with passive screen_time to estimate objective durations.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; inspect for episodic spikes and consider threshold/event flags

criteria_variables.label: Acute panic/anxiety when deadlines are noticed

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how anxious or panicked did you feel specifically because of deadlines or time pressure? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Extremely)
measurement.notes: If a deadline-triggered panic attack occurred, rate intensity; also optionally flag event in free comment.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-scoring; consider event-flag modeling for extreme spikes

criteria_variables.label: Uncertainty and self-blame about cause of difficulties

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel unsure if this was laziness versus a fixable problem, and blame yourself? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Strongly uncertain/blaming)
measurement.notes: Captures rumination/self-judgment which can block selecting strategies.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person centering and z-score

predictor_variables.label: Implementation intentions / task-initiation cues
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Specific if-then plans (e.g., 'If I sit down at X, I will do Y for 5 minutes') reduce intention–action gap and cue initiation rapidly.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Create a brief if-then plan before work (e.g., 'When I open my laptop, I will spend 5 minutes on step 1').

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you form a specific if-then plan to start the next task? (1 = Not at all specific, 9 = Very specific/actionable)
measurement.notes: Directly captures planning strength rather than presence/absence.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Forms immediate cue that reduces failure to initiate within hours.
Reduces probability of substituting with easy rewards by increasing commitment to stepwise starts.
A clear plan focuses attention and reduces distractibility in the near term.
May reduce off-task drifting and subsequent time loss across the work period.
Successful initiation from plans can reduce deadline-triggered panic by decreasing accrued delay.
Actionable plans can reduce self-blame by producing observable change.

feasibility.notes: Short EMA item; likely good compliance.

predictor_variables.label: Structured focus blocks and micro-task timers
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Using timers and graded micro-tasks supports sustained attention and reduces switching to substitutes.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Commit to a 25-minute timer or a 10-minute micro-task with a visible countdown.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you use a timer or micro-task structure to focus? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Consistently used and adhered to)
measurement.notes: Captures use and adherence rather than theoretical awareness.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Timers encourage action immediately and sustain initial engagement.
Reduces substitution by structuring work into small achievable chunks.
Buffering attention with focused blocks reduces distractibility.
May reduce prolonged off-task episodes and time loss.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA question; actual timer usage may be validated by third-party app logs if available.

predictor_variables.label: Digital distraction (passive phone metrics: screen time and pickups)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: High screen-time and frequent pickups indicate environmental/digital drivers of substitution and time loss; reducing pickups/screen time reduces distraction.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Enable Do Not Disturb, scheduled app limits, remove tempting apps from home screen.

measurement.channel: smartphone OS usage stats (screen_time_minutes, unlock_count) aggregated to EMA windows
measurement.item_or_signal: screen_time_minutes in past ~3 hours; unlock_count in past ~3 hours (collected via OS usage API; content not captured)
measurement.notes: Use privacy-preserving aggregation (minutes and counts). Normalize per-person for gVAR.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p for minutes; within-person z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Higher recent screen time strongly correlates with substitution behavior.
Pickups and screen minutes directly index distractibility.
More distraction increases failure to initiate/continue intended tasks.
Longer screen sessions predict loss of time awareness.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure explicit consent for passive collection; avoid content-level monitoring.

feasibility.notes: OS-level access required; participant permissions and battery constraints may reduce sampling.

predictor_variables.label: Time-awareness checks / time-estimation practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Frequent clock-checks, countdowns or brief time-estimation practices recalibrate subjective time and reduce unnoticed long off-task periods.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Set visible countdown timers, pause to estimate elapsed time every 10–20 minutes.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how often did you check the time or use a countdown to monitor elapsed time? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Constantly)
measurement.notes: Can be augmented with clock-app open counts if available.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: More frequent time checks directly reduce loss of time awareness in the near term.
Better time-awareness supports timely initiation and return to task.
Reduces long immersion in substitutes by prompting re-evaluation of elapsed time.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA item; clock-app opens may be noisy across device types.

predictor_variables.label: Contingency planning / deadline reframing (cognitive restructuring)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Pre-emptive planning and cognitive reframing reduce acute deadline-triggered panic by converting threat into manageable steps.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Brief reappraisal of deadline meaning (e.g., 'I can prioritize the next immediate step') and if-then contingency plans.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you do any contingency planning or reframe upcoming deadlines (e.g., plan next steps, reappraise) ? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Very much)
measurement.notes: Targets the cognitive step expected to reduce panic when deadlines appear.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Reappraisal and contingency plans directly reduce acute deadline-related anxiety in the short term.
Contingency planning may increase perceived ability to act, thereby improving initiation (sign may be indirect; increased planning can precede action).
Reduces uncertainty/self-blame by creating concrete next steps.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Cognitive restructuring items could transiently raise distress if recalling stress; ensure supportive instructions.

feasibility.notes: Short EMA question acceptable; therapist-guided training may increase effect sizes later.

predictor_variables.label: Self-compassion / uncertainty-processing practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Self-compassion reduces self-blame and rumination, supporting adaptive selection of corrective steps and reducing avoidance.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short self-compassion prompts (e.g., 'What would I say to a friend in this situation?').

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you practice self-compassion or intentionally reduce self-blame? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Very much)
measurement.notes: Captures active practice (not trait self-compassion).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Directly targets self-blame and uncertainty, reducing their intensity.
Reduces emotional reactivity to deadlines via affect regulation.
By lowering avoidance, may indirectly improve initiation (effect may be indirect).

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: May briefly surface negative self-evaluations; framing should be supportive.

feasibility.notes: Short prompts have high acceptability.

predictor_variables.label: External cognitive offloading / checklists
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Using checklists or stepwise external supports reduces reliance on internal working memory and lowers activation energy for task starts.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Use a 3-step checklist posted near work space (e.g., open doc, write title, write first sentence).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you use an external checklist or offloading tool to start/structure the task? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Used and followed closely)
measurement.notes: Designed to capture external scaffolding use.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Offloading reduces friction to initiating tasks.
Structured steps channel behavior away from substitutes.
Tangible progress reduces self-blame via feedback.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA item; observable behavior.

predictor_variables.label: Current energy / alertness level
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Momentary energy/alertness moderates ability to initiate and resist low-effort substitutes; influenced by sleep, nutrition and circadian factors.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short behavioral changes: hydration, brief movement break, or short nap (outside work contexts) to restore energy.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now (since the last prompt), how would you rate your energy/alertness? (1 = Exhausted, 9 = Very high energy)
measurement.notes: Energy is a proximal moderator for initiation and distractibility.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; consider controlling for circadian time-of-day

expected_effects.rationale: Higher energy is expected to reduce failure to initiate in the short term.
Low energy increases reliance on low-effort substitutes.
Better energy improves sustained attention against digital pulls.

feasibility.notes: Short momentary item with high compliance expected.

predictor_variables.label: Last-night sleep quality and duration (morning diary)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Sleep influences daytime cognitive control and energy; poor sleep can increase distractibility and reduce initiation.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Consistent sleep schedule, sleep hygiene changes (no electronics before bed), environmental adjustments.

measurement.channel: morning diary in smartphone EMA app
measurement.item_or_signal: Last night: how many hours did you sleep? ______ minutes. How would you rate your sleep quality? (1 = Very poor, 9 = Very good)
measurement.notes: Use duration + quality; primary lag expected 0-24h to daytime outcomes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: duration: none or standardized; quality: z-score; control for weekday effects

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces failure to initiate across the next day.
Improves self-control against low-effort substitutions the following day.
Improves sustained attention.
Better sleep reduces time-loss episodes.

feasibility.notes: Single morning diary item; high expected completion if kept brief.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 (implementation intentions), P02 (structured focus), and P07 (checklists) are conceptually related and may show moderate collinearity.
P06 (self-compassion) and C06 (self-blame) will be correlated; careful temporal ordering needed.
P03 passive phone metrics might correlate with EMA reports of distraction (C03), requiring careful modeling to avoid measurement redundancy.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: Passive screen_time minutes could be zero during some windows (floor).
Highly motivated participants may show ceiling responses on implementation-intention or checklist items, reducing variance.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1–9 Likert anchors with behavioral anchors to increase spread.
Aggregate passive minutes over 3-hour windows to reduce zero-inflation but preserve variability.
Encourage participants to report brief, concrete episodes rather than binary yes/no to avoid floor effects.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Biomedical/neurostimulation interventions (e.g., TMS, pharmacotherapy) deprioritized at this initial behavioral gVAR stage due to scope and safety.
Highly overlapping executive-function subnodes (e.g., many fine-grained planning variants) collapsed into implementation intentions/structured focus to reduce redundancy.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 28 days and 6 prompts/day (168 max momentary prompts) plus 28 morning diaries = 196 total expected observations, expect ~100–150 usable timepoints after typical missingness. For a single-person gVAR with 15 nodes, regularization (LASSO/graphical-LASSO) or Bayesian shrinkage is recommended. Consider reducing nodes or combining highly collinear predictors if realized T is substantially below target.

safety_notes: No acute self-harm or severe psychiatric risk is evident in the complaint text. If panic episodes are frequent/intense or there is functional impairment beyond typical distress, advise contacting a mental health professional. Passive collection must follow privacy/consent rules (no content capture). Avoid recommending medication changes; if pharmacologic treatment is suspected necessary, refer to a clinician.
